---
title: "Government Employment & the Public Service"
description: "How many Canadians work for government — by level, and inside the federal public service"
---

Governments are large employers. This page asks a deceptively simple question —
**how many people work for government, and doing what** — and answers it from the
ground up: first across all levels of government, then drilling into the federal
public service. A companion page covers [federal spending](spending.qmd), and the
[Public Finances](../fiscal/index.qmd) page compares Canada's government finances
with OECD peers.

Two definitions matter throughout, because they are easy to conflate:

- **Public administration** — the "core bureaucracy": people administering
  government programs (NAICS sector 91). It does **not** include public-sector
  teachers, nurses, or professors, who are counted in the education and health
  industries.
- **The public sector** — the broader universe: general government **plus**
  health and social-service institutions, school boards, universities and
  colleges, and government business enterprises (Crown corporations). It is
  several times larger than public administration alone.

The size of government and how it is staffed are matters of legitimate political
debate. This page takes no position on whether government is "too big" or "too
small": it reports counts, shares, peer benchmarks, and official definitions, and
lets the reader judge.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
from pipeline.config import get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import (peer_comparison_line, ranked_bar, single_line,
                             single_line_multi, lines_over_time, stacked_area, category_bar)
```

## Employment by Level of Government

The chart below counts **jobs in public administration** — the core bureaucracy of
each level of government — back to 1997. Local government (municipalities and
regional/Indigenous administrations) and the federal government are the largest;
provincial and territorial public administration is smaller, because so much of
what provinces *fund* — schools, hospitals, universities — is delivered by
institutions counted separately (see the next chart). The federal line includes
National Defence, including the military; federal **civilian** public
administration is roughly a quarter smaller.

```{python}
#| echo: false
lvl_path = "data/government/statcan_govt_employment_by_level.csv"
if os.path.exists(lvl_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(lvl_path)
    keep = ["Federal", "Provincial / territorial", "Local & Indigenous"]
    fig = lines_over_time(
        df[df["level"].isin(keep)], "year", "jobs", "level",
        yaxis_title="Number of jobs",
        colors={"Federal": "#d62728", "Provincial / territorial": "#4e79a7",
                "Local & Indigenous": "#59a14f"},
        group_order=keep,
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada 36-10-0489-01 (SNA jobs) — public administration (NAICS 91); federal includes the military | Data as of: {get_data_date(lvl_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

## The Bureaucracy vs. the Whole Public Sector

"Government workers" are far more than the bureaucracy. The Labour Force Survey
counts everyone whose employer is a government, a public institution, or a
government business enterprise — about **4.5 million people** in 2025. Grouped by
industry, the two largest parts are **public education** (teachers and professors)
and **public health care and social services** (nurses, hospital and long-term-care
staff) — each employing *more* people than **public administration**, the core
bureaucracy across all three levels of government combined. Crown corporations and
other public services (utilities, public transit, and the like) make up the rest.

```{python}
#| echo: false
comp_path = "data/government/statcan_public_sector_composition.csv"
PILLARS = {"Educational services": "Educational services",
           "Health care and social assistance": "Health care & social assistance",
           "Public administration": "Public administration (the bureaucracy)"}
OTHER = "Other (Crown corporations, utilities, transit, etc.)"
if os.path.exists(comp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(comp_path)
    df["bucket"] = df["sector"].map(PILLARS).fillna(OTHER)
    area = df.groupby(["year", "bucket"], as_index=False)["employment"].sum()
    order = ["Educational services", "Health care & social assistance",
             "Public administration (the bureaucracy)", OTHER]
    fig = stacked_area(area, "year", "employment", "bucket",
        yaxis_title="Public-sector employees (thousands)", value_scale=1000, hover_decimals=0,
        group_order=order, legend_orientation="h", height=560,
        colors={"Educational services": "#4e79a7", "Health care & social assistance": "#59a14f",
                "Public administration (the bureaucracy)": "#f28e2b", OTHER: "#bab0ac"},
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada 14-10-0027-01 (Labour Force Survey, public-sector employees by industry) | Data as of: {get_data_date(comp_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

Ranked for the latest year, public administration — the part most people picture as
"government" — is only the third-largest part of the public-sector workforce, behind
public education and public health care:

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(comp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(comp_path)
    yr = int(df["year"].max())
    latest = df[df["year"] == yr].copy()
    latest["sector"] = latest["sector"].replace(
        {"Health care and social assistance": "Health care & social assistance"})
    fig = category_bar(latest, "sector", "employment", top_n=10,
        xaxis_title=f"Public-sector employees (thousands, {yr})", value_scale=1000, value_fmt=",.0f",
        highlight={"Public administration"}, highlight_color="#f28e2b",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada 14-10-0027-01 (Labour Force Survey) | Data as of: {get_data_date(comp_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

::: {.callout-note}
## How "the public sector" is defined here
This uses the Labour Force Survey's **class of worker**: a public-sector employee is
anyone whose employer is a government department, a public institution (public
schools, hospitals, universities), or a government business enterprise (Crown
corporation). Broken down by **industry**, "public administration" is the
civil-service bureaucracy of all three levels combined — the federal/provincial/
local split is the chart above. (This replaces an older institutional-sector table
that Statistics Canada discontinued in 2011.)
:::

## How Canada Compares

Across the OECD, the share of total employment that works for **general
government** (all levels combined, including public health and education staff)
varies widely — from around 5% in Japan and South Korea to nearly 30% in the
Nordic countries. Canada sits a little above the middle of its peer group. How
large this share "should" be is a political choice, so it is shown without a
favourable direction.

```{python}
#| echo: false
emp_path = "data/government/oecd_govt_employment.csv"
if os.path.exists(emp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(emp_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "govt_emp_pct", title="",
        yaxis_title="% of total employment", show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Government at a Glance 2025 (general government employment) | Data as of: {get_data_date(emp_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.52, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(emp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(emp_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "govt_emp_pct", "General government employment (% of total employment)",
        f"Source: OECD Government at a Glance 2025 | Data as of: {get_data_date(emp_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}%<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

# Inside the Federal Public Service

The rest of this page narrows to the **federal public service** as Treasury Board
counts it: the core public administration plus separate agencies (such as the
Canada Revenue Agency and Parks Canada), measured as a headcount on March 31 each
year. It **excludes** the Canadian Armed Forces and RCMP regular and civilian
members, who are reported under their own frameworks.

## Total Headcount Over Time

The federal public service has moved in waves: growth through the late 2000s, a
contraction after the 2012 budget's spending review, and renewed growth since,
reaching about 358,000 in 2025. Drag the **slider** to zoom to a period, and use
the **dropdown** to switch between the total headcount and the number **per 1,000
people** — which has risen far less, because Canada's population has grown quickly
over the same years.

```{python}
#| echo: false
pop_path = "data/government/tbs_fps_population.csv"
canpop_path = "data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv"
if os.path.exists(pop_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(pop_path)
    t = df[df["segment"] == "Total"].copy()
    t["date"] = pd.to_datetime(t["year"], format="%Y")
    opts = [dict(col="employees", label="Total headcount",
                 yaxis_title="Employees (March 31)",
                 hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f} employees<extra></extra>")]
    if os.path.exists(canpop_path):                 # add the per-capita view
        cp = pd.read_csv(canpop_path)
        cp = cp[cp["geography"] == "Canada"].copy()
        cp["year"] = pd.to_datetime(cp["date"]).dt.year
        popyr = cp.groupby("year")["population"].mean()
        t["per_1000"] = [e / popyr[y] * 1000 if y in popyr.index else None
                         for e, y in zip(t["employees"], t["year"])]
        opts.append(dict(col="per_1000", label="Per 1,000 population",
                         yaxis_title="Federal public servants per 1,000 people",
                         hovertemplate="%{y:.2f} per 1,000 people<extra></extra>"))
    fig = single_line_multi(t, "date", opts, rangeslider=True,
        source_note=f"Source: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat — Federal public service statistics (core public administration + separate agencies); population from Statistics Canada 17-10-0009-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(pop_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

## Which Parts of the Public Service Grew

The public service added roughly **75,000 people between 2010 and 2025**. The
increase was concentrated in **benefits and service delivery** — Employment and
Social Development Canada, which runs Service Canada, and the Canada Revenue Agency
— together with **immigration** (Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada and
the Immigration and Refugee Board) and **procurement, border, and security**
organizations.

```{python}
#| echo: false
dgrow_path = "data/government/tbs_fps_by_department.csv"
if os.path.exists(dgrow_path):
    dep = pd.read_csv(dgrow_path)
    y0, y1 = int(dep["year"].min()), int(dep["year"].max())
    a = dep[dep["year"] == y0].set_index("department")["employees"]
    b = dep[dep["year"] == y1].set_index("department")["employees"]
    common = a.index.intersection(b.index)
    chg = (b.reindex(common) - a.reindex(common)).reset_index()
    chg.columns = ["department", "change"]
    chg = chg[chg["change"] > 0]
    fig = category_bar(chg, "department", "change", top_n=12,
        xaxis_title=f"Increase in employees, {y0}→{y1}", value_fmt=",.0f",
        source_note=f"Source: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat — Federal public service statistics | Data as of: {get_data_date(dgrow_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## On "areas," and on reorganizations
Federal jobs are not published by occupation or role over time, so this shows
growth by **department** — the closest available view of *where* the workforce
expanded. It compares departments that kept the same name across the period;
machinery-of-government changes that created or split organizations (for example
**Shared Services Canada** in 2011, and the 2017 split that created **Indigenous
Services Canada**) shift staff between departments and so do not appear as "growth"
here.
:::

## Where Federal Public Servants Work

The largest federal employers are the service-delivery and operational
organizations — the Canada Revenue Agency, Employment and Social Development
Canada (which runs Service Canada), National Defence's civilian workforce,
Correctional Service Canada, and the Canada Border Services Agency. Because
departments are periodically renamed, merged, and split, this is shown as a
current-year snapshot rather than a time series.

```{python}
#| echo: false
dept_path = "data/government/tbs_fps_by_department.csv"
if os.path.exists(dept_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(dept_path)
    latest = df[df["year"] == df["year"].max()]
    yr = int(df["year"].max())
    fig = category_bar(latest, "department", "employees", top_n=15,
        xaxis_title=f"Employees ({yr})", value_fmt=",.0f",
        source_note=f"Source: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat — Federal public service statistics | Data as of: {get_data_date(dept_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

## The Changing Shape of the Workforce

Over time the federal public service has shifted toward more senior,
knowledge-intensive work. One measure available as a consistent series is the
**executive** cadre (the EX group): it has grown faster than the workforce as a
whole and now makes up a little over 2.5% of all employees.

```{python}
#| echo: false
exe_path = "data/government/infobase_fps_executive.csv"
if os.path.exists(exe_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(exe_path)
    piv = df.pivot_table(index="year", columns="category", values="employees").reset_index()
    piv["share"] = piv["Executive"] / (piv["Executive"] + piv["Non-executive"]) * 100
    piv["date"] = pd.to_datetime(piv["year"], format="%Y")
    fig = single_line(piv, "date", "share", title="",
        yaxis_title="Executives (% of employees)",
        hovertemplate="%{y:.2f}% executives<extra></extra>",
        source_note=f"Source: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat (GC InfoBase), employee population by executive level | Data as of: {get_data_date(exe_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What about scientists, economists, and front-line roles?
Federal jobs are classified into [occupational groups](https://www.canada.ca/en/treasury-board-secretariat/services/collective-agreements/occupational-groups.html)
— for example **EC** (Economics and Social Science Services, the economists and
social-science researchers), **SE/AP** (the scientific and applied-science/
engineering groups), **CS/IT** (information technology), **FB** (Border Services),
**PA** (Program and Administrative Services — much of the front-line and
administrative workforce), and **EX** (Executive).

A clean, comparable count of these groups *over time* is, unfortunately, **not
published as machine-readable open data**, and the classification itself has been
reorganized repeatedly (the occupational *categories* were repealed in 1993; the
1999 Universal Classification Standard consolidated some 72 groups into 29; the CS
group was later converted to IT). Those breaks make a naive long-run series
misleading, which is why this page does not chart one.

The direction of travel is, however, well documented. According to Treasury
Board's [Demographic Snapshot of the Federal Public Service, 2017](https://www.canada.ca/en/treasury-board-secretariat/services/innovation/human-resources-statistics/demographic-snapshot-federal-public-service-2017.html),
the five largest knowledge-intensive groups (AS, PM, CS, EC and EX) grew from
**31.8% of the core public administration in 2000 to 42.6% in 2017**, and the
executive workforce grew **56%** over that period — consistent with the trend in
the chart above.
:::

## Who the Public Service Is

```{python}
#| echo: false
demo_path = "data/government/tbs_fps_demographics.csv"
demo = pd.read_csv(demo_path) if os.path.exists(demo_path) else None
demo_yr = int(demo["year"].max()) if demo is not None else None
demo_src = f"Source: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat — Federal public service statistics | Data as of: {get_data_date(demo_path)}" if demo is not None else ""
```

**Ageing.** Like much of the workforce, the federal public service has a large
share of employees in their 40s and 50s — a wave of eventual retirements.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if demo is not None:
    order = ['<20', '20-24', '25-29', '30-34', '35-39', '40-44', '45-49',
             '50-54', '55-59', '60-64', '65+']
    age = demo[(demo["dimension"] == "Age band") & (demo["category"] != "Unknown")]
    fig = stacked_area(age, "year", "employees", "category",
        yaxis_title="Employees (thousands)", value_scale=1000, group_order=order,
        source_note=demo_src)
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

**Tenure.** The large majority hold indeterminate (permanent) positions; terms,
casuals, and students make up a smaller, more variable share.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if demo is not None:
    ten = demo[(demo["dimension"] == "Tenure") & (demo["category"] != "Unknown")]
    fig = stacked_area(ten, "year", "employees", "category",
        yaxis_title="Employees (thousands)", value_scale=1000,
        group_order=["Indeterminate", "Term", "Casual", "Student"], source_note=demo_src)
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

**Where they work.** The National Capital Region (Ottawa–Gatineau) is the single
largest concentration — larger than any one province — but most public servants
work outside it, delivering services across the country.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if demo is not None:
    reg = demo[(demo["dimension"] == "Region") & (~demo["category"].isin(["Unknown"]))
               & (demo["year"] == demo_yr)]
    fig = category_bar(reg, "category", "employees", xaxis_title=f"Employees ({demo_yr})",
        value_fmt=",.0f", highlight={"National Capital Region (NCR)"}, source_note=demo_src)
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

**Sex.** Women have been a majority of the federal public service throughout the
series and their share has edged up.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if demo is not None:
    sx = demo[(demo["dimension"] == "Sex") & (demo["category"] != "Unknown")]
    fig = lines_over_time(sx, "year", "employees", "category", yaxis_title="Employees",
        colors={"Women": "#b07aa1", "Men": "#4e79a7"}, group_order=["Women", "Men"],
        source_note=demo_src)
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Download Data

- [Government employment by level (CSV)](../data/government/statcan_govt_employment_by_level.csv) — Statistics Canada 36-10-0489-01
- [Public sector by institutional sector (CSV)](../data/government/statcan_public_sector_composition.csv) — Statistics Canada 10-10-0025-01 (archived)
- [General government employment vs OECD peers (CSV)](../data/government/oecd_govt_employment.csv) — OECD Government at a Glance
- [Federal public service population (CSV)](../data/government/tbs_fps_population.csv) — Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
- [Federal public service by department (CSV)](../data/government/tbs_fps_by_department.csv) — Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
- [Federal public service demographics (CSV)](../data/government/tbs_fps_demographics.csv) — Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
- [Federal public service executives (CSV)](../data/government/infobase_fps_executive.csv) — GC InfoBase
